entry.title: Clean Core approach with the ASAPIO Clean Core Check
entry.content:
Introduction 
In this blog, I am going to show how to use the ASAPIO Integration Add-on Clean Core Check as a proactive safeguard to ensure Clean Core compliance – without manually researching released objects, SAP Notes, or successor APIs. 

The idea behind the Clean Core Check is simple: whenever a payload or outbound object is defined, the framework validates whether the used tables or objects are released for usage in SAP S/4HANA (especially in cloud-ready or clean core scenarios). If not, it automatically suggests alternative released CDS views. 

In short: instead of discovering during an upgrade that a table like VBAK is not released, you detect it during design time – and receive a proposal such as I_SALESDOCUMENT as successor. 

This approach requires the ASAPIO Integration Add-on and is particularly relevant in S/4HANA environments where unreleased objects must be avoided. 

 

Why Clean Core matters 

SAP’s Clean Core strategy aims to: 

  • Reduce modification footprint 
  • Prevent direct access to non-released database tables 
  • Ensure upgrade stability 
  • Enforce usage of released APIs and CDS views 

Many classical SAP tables (e.g. VBAK, VBKD, VEDA) are technically available but not released for direct usage in S/4HANA Cloud or Clean Core compliant developments. 

Using them in custom extraction logic or event payloads creates technical debt and upgrade risk. 

This is exactly where the ASAPIO Clean Core Check comes into play. 

 

Example: Payload with classic SD tables 

Let’s assume we defined a Sales Order payload in the ASAPIO Payload Designer (transaction /ASADEV/DESIGN). 

We add the following tables: 

  • VBAK – Sales Document Header 
  • VBPA – Partner Data 
  • VEDA – Contract Data 
  • VBKD – Business Data 

The payload is configured and released. 

Now, instead of discovering issues during a system conversion or ATC run, we execute the Clean Core Check directly in the Payload Designer. 

 

Running the Clean Core Check 

Inside the Payload Designer, simply click on the Clean Core Check button in the toolbar. 

The framework evaluates all tables and objects used in the payload definition. 

 

 

What happens here? 

  • The system detects that VBAK, VBKD, and VEDA are not released objects 
  • It checks SAP’s released API model 
  • It proposes I_SALESDOCUMENT as an alternative CDS View 
  • It flags objects not listed in released content (e.g. VBPA) 

This happens instantly. 

 

Enabling the Clean Core Check  

Before the Clean Core Check can be executed, the corresponding repository configuration must be maintained. 

The check relies on the ASAPIO Clean Core repository, which contains the metadata about released and non-released SAP objects as well as successor CDS views. 

To activate and configure the check properly, follow the official documentation: 

https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1#use_clean_core_checks 

 

Refactoring to released CDS views 

After the Clean Core Check result, the next step is straightforward: 

  1. Remove the unreleased tables from the payload 
  1. Replace them with the suggested CDS view 
  1. Re-map the required fields 

In our example, instead of selecting from VBAK directly, we use CDS View I_SALESDOCUMENT. 

Benefits: 

  • Released API contract 
  • Semantic layer abstraction 
  • Upgrade stability 
  • Cloud readiness 
  • Compliance with Clean Core guidelines 

The payload can now be executed without violating SAP’s release strategy. 

 

Summary 

The ASAPIO Clean Core Check provides: 

  • Immediate detection of non-released tables 
  • Automatic suggestion of released CDS alternatives 
  • Support for SAP Clean Core strategy 
  • Reduced upgrade risks 
  • Faster development cycles 

Instead of reacting to ATC findings during system conversion, developers can proactively design compliant payloads from the beginning. 

It shifts Clean Core validation directly into the design phase. 

In other words: 

Configuration-driven integration, now also Clean Core compliant. 

Let me know if you would like to see a deeper technical walkthrough or how the check is implemented internally in the Add-on.

entry.title: How to use BPA API Trigger using Fiori
entry.content:
Introduction: How to make your Fiori app talk to SAP Build Process Automation (BPA)? Well, I'm going to show you exactly how to trigger BPA workflows directly from your Fiori application using API triggers.
Think of it like this: your user fills out a form in Fiori, hits submit, this enables communication between Fiori and BPA, allowing your workflow to start automatically based on form input.
 
What we're building today:
  1. A Fiori form that captures sales order data
  2. A BPA workflow that gets triggered automatically
  3. The connection between them using API triggers

An API trigger - It is a BPA allows external systems such as SAP Fiori, CPI, or any REST capable application to start a process by calling an API endpoint.

Part 1: Creating the BPA Automation with API Trigger

1. Go to BTP Lobby -> Create - > Automated Process -> Process.
You’ll see a new process canvas with an option to add a trigger.

2. Click API Trigger to set this as the starting point of your workflow.

3. Give a suitable name and click Create.

4. Add a Data Type that contains the fields you want to receive from Fiori.

5. Create a custom variable using the same Data Type created above.

6. Your workflow might look like this:

API Trigger
Condition (e.g., check if Net Amount > 1000)
-> If Yes ->  Auto Approval
-> If No -> User Approval
->  Acknowledgement Form or Rejection Form

Once ready, Save → Release → Deploy the process.

Note: After deployment only it will appear in Environment 

7. Go to Control Tower -> Environments -> Public (or your selected environment).
Click the three dots -> View.

8. Below is the sample JSON payload which it used to map values 

Copy this  you’ll use it in your Fiori app.

Part 2: Creating the Fiori App (Freestyle SAPUI5)

1. Open BAS -> File -> New Project from Template.

2. Select SAP Fiori generator and click start.

3. Select basic to create freestyle application and click next.

 

4. select Data Source and Service Selection

  • Data Source: Connect to a system

  • System: Choose your SAP system

  • Service: Select the OData service

Click Next.

5. Select yes for deployment configuration and click next.

6. Deployment configuration

  • Target -> Cloud Foundry

  • Destination Name -> Your destination

  • Add Router Module -> Managed Application Router

Finish the wizard.

7. In Cloud Foundry sign in and targets Choose SSO Passcode.
Click the link ->  Select Identity Provider -> Enter Passcode -> Sign in.

Click the URL, and it will take you to the Identity Provider selection page. Choose the default provider, verify that your user ID is correct, and continue. A passcode will be generated copy it, paste it and sign in.

8. simple Fiori form 

View1.view.xml

<mvc:View controllerName="salesorder.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:l="sap.ui.layout"
    xmlns:f="sap.ui.layout.form">

    <Page id="page" title="Sales Order Form">

        <VBox id="_IDGenVBox1" class="sapUiSmallMargin"   alignItems="Center">

            <!-- Centered container with limited width -->
            <VBox id="_IDGenVBox2" width="50%" class="sapUiSmallMarginAuto"  >

                <f:SimpleForm id="_IDGenSimpleForm"
                    editable="true"
                    layout="ResponsiveGridLayout"
                    labelSpanL="4" labelSpanM="4"
                    adjustLabelSpan="false"
                    emptySpanL="1" emptySpanM="1"
                    columnsL="1" columnsM="1">

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel" text="Sales Order"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput" value="{salesorderdetails/SalesOrder}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel1" text="Sales Org"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput1" value="{salesorderdetails/SalesOrg}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel2" text="Distribution Channel"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput2" value="{salesorderdetails/DistributionChannel}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel3" text="Division"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput3" value="{salesorderdetails/Division}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel4" text="Document Date"/>
                    <DatePicker id="_IDGenDatePicker" value="{salesorderdetails/DocumentDate}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel5" text="Created By"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput4" value="{salesorderdetails/CreatedByUser}"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel6" text="Customer Number"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput5" value="{salesorderdetails/CustomerNumber}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel7" text="Order Amount"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput6" value="{salesorderdetails/OrderAmount}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel8" text="Purchase Order Number"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput7" value="{salesorderdetails/PurchaseOrderNumber}" required="true"/>

                </f:SimpleForm>

                <!-- Submit button aligned to right -->
                <HBox id="_IDGenHBox" justifyContent="End" class="sapUiSmallMarginTop">
                    <Button id="_IDGenButton" text="Submit" type="Emphasized" press=".onSubmit"/>
                </HBox>

            </VBox>

        </VBox>

    </Page>

</mvc:View>

View1.controller.js

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], (Controller) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("salesorder.controller.View1", {
        onInit() {
        },
        
         _getExternalServiceRuntimeBaseURL: function () {
            var oComponent = sap.ui.core.Component.getOwnerComponentFor(this.getView());
                if (oComponent) {
                    var sAppId = oComponent.getManifestEntry("/sap.app/id");
                    var sAppPath = sAppId.replaceAll(".", "/");
                    var sAppModulePath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath(sAppPath);
                    return sAppModulePath;
                } else {
                    console.error("Component could not be found.");
                    return "";
                }
    
        },
        onSubmit() {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var url = "/sap_process_automation_service/workflow/rest/v1/workflow-instances";
            var sUrl = this._getExternalServiceRuntimeBaseURL() + url;
            try {
                var payload = {
                    "definitionId": "eu10.fin-dev-project.salesorder522.salesOrder",
                        "context": {
                        "salesorderdetails": {
                            "SalesOrder":  oView.byId("_IDGenInput").getValue(),
                                "SalesOrg": oView.byId("_IDGenInput1").getValue(),
                                    "DistributionChannel": oView.byId("_IDGenInput3").getValue(),
                                        "Division": oView.byId("_IDGenInput2").getValue(),
                                            "DocumentDate": String(oView.byId("_IDGenDatePicker").getValue() || ""),
                                                "CreatedByUser": oView.byId("_IDGenInput4").getValue(),
                                                    "CustomerNumber": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput5").getValue()||""),
                                                        "OrderAmount": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput6").getValue() || ""),
                                                            "PurchaseOrderNumber": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput7").getValue() || "")
                        }
                    }
                }
                console.log("Payload: ", payload);
                  $.ajax({
                url: sUrl,
                type: "POST",
                dataType: "json",
                contentType: "application/json",
                data: JSON.stringify(payload),
                beforeSend: function (xhr) {
                    xhr.setRequestHeader("Accept", "application/json");
                },
                success: function (response) {
                    console.log("Success: ", response);
                }.bind(this),
                error: function (error) {
                    console.log("Error in POST call:", error);
                }

            });

            } catch (error) {
                console.error("Submission failed:", error);
            }
        }
    });
});

xs.app.json -

//add this in routes   
   {
      "source": "^/process_automation_service/(.*)$",
      "target": "$1",
      "destination": "process_automation_service",
      "authenticationType": "xsuaa",
      "csrfProtection": false
    }

9. Right-click mta.yaml -> Build MTA Project.
This generates an .mtar file in mta_archives.

10. Right-click the .mtar file -> Deploy MTA Archive.

11. Go to BTP cockpit -> HTML5 Applications -> click your app.

12. Fill the form -> Click Submit.
you will get success message in console

If it fails, check:

  • Destination configuration

  • xs-app.json routes

  • BPA credentials

13. Once triggered, the workflow reaches the approver in the BPA Inbox.

14. you will receive the acknowledge form once you received you can accept it.

15. Once everything is over you will see the completed status

16 Click the instance to see:

  • step details

  • submitted data

  • log

Note: In BPA, "Started By" usually shows the SAP user.
But when triggering through external systems (like Fiori), it may generate a system string instead of the real user ID.

So design your workflow logic accordingly.

Conclusion:  You've successfully built a seamless integration between SAP Fiori and SAP Build Process Automation.

What you accomplished:

  • Created an end-to-end automated workflow from Fiori form submission to BPA approval process
  • Implemented real-time API communication using REST triggers
  • Established a reusable integration pattern for future business processes

You're combining the user-friendly interface of Fiori with the powerful automation capabilities of BPA, while maintaining full connectivity to your core ABAP systems - creating a comprehensive end-to-end solution.

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: Solution architect - SAP Business Technology Platform
entry.content:
Every great solution architecture usually starts the same way: a messy whiteboard, a few big business goals, and the question… how do we design this the right way?

If you’re working with SAP Business Technology Platform, you’ve probably been there.

That’s why we’re hosting the Partner learning accelerator: Solution architect – SAP BTP, a 90-minute live session designed for partners preparing for the SAP BTP Solution Architect certification.

We’ll break down what strong architecture really looks like across SAP BTP.  Not just theory. Real considerations: how to translate business requirements into technical blueprints, how integration strategies fit into enterprise landscapes, and where governance, DevOps, analytics, and master data play their role.

The goal is simple: help experienced partners connect the architectural dots faster and approach solution design with more clarity and confidence.

If you’re already building on SAP BTP and thinking about the next step in your architect journey… this session will be time well spent.

Register now and join the conversation.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/nov/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00012265.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #EnterpriseArchitecture #CloudStrategy #PartnerEnablement

entry.title: My SAP Business AI Certification Journey
entry.content:
When I started these SAP Business AI courses (Discovering SAP Business AI, Positioning SAP Business AI and Boosting AI-driven Business Transformation with Joule Agents), I expected a standard product overview. What I got instead was a completely new way of thinking about enterprise technology, AI strategy, and how to have meaningful conversations with clients about digital transformation.

This blog is my attempt to share what I learned, how I passed my certification, and what surprised me along the way — for anyone about to take the same journey.

SAP Certification Name: SAP Certified - Positioning SAP Business AI Solutions as part of SAP Business Suite (C_BCBAI)

1. What the Certification Covers

The certification spans three SAP Learning courses, each building on the previous one. Together they cover the full SAP Business AI story — from strategy to technical architecture to real-world deployment.

Course

Focus

Key Learning

Discovering SAP Business AI

Why AI? What is SAP's strategy?

The SAP Flywheel — how Applications, Data and AI reinforce each other

Positioning SAP Business AI

How to position AI to different personas

Joule, AI Foundation, Responsible AI, Sustainability — and how each lands with CIO, CFO, COO

Joule Agents & Transformation

How AI agents actually work in business

Agentic AI, Business Data Cloud, Knowledge Graph, AI Agent Hub — the full technical picture

2. The Three Things That Changed How I Think

A. AI Without Data Is Just Noise

The biggest insight from Course 1 was simple but profound: AI is only as good as the data feeding it. Before these courses, I used to talk about AI features. Now I start every conversation with the data foundation.

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is what makes AI reliable — connecting all systems using zero-copy federation, preserving business context through the Semantic Layer, and keeping data sovereign in each region. Without BDC, AI outputs are guesswork.

B. Joule Is Not Just a Chatbot

Before the certification, I described Joule as "SAP's AI assistant." After it, I describe it very differently. Joule is an omnipresent AI Copilot with 1,800+ skills, covering 80% of SAP's most-used transactions, available in 11 languages, and capable of proactively acting before users even ask.

And then there are Joule Agents — a completely different level. Agents reason, plan, and execute multi-step workflows autonomously. The Cash Management Agent saves finance teams up to 70% of manual reconciliation time. The Production Planning Agent autonomously checks material and capacity. 

C. Responsible AI Is a Commercial Accelerator

I used to treat ethics and compliance as a defensive topic — something to address when clients raised concerns. The certification changed that. SAP's Responsible AI framework is actually a competitive advantage.

  • Client data never leaves SAP's governed architecture — never used to train third-party models
  • Every Joule action is fully auditable — no black-box decisions
  • Data sovereignty built in — GDPR in Europe, PDPA in Asia, automatic
  • ISO 42001 certified for AI governance — internationally recognised standard
  • Human-in-the-loop by design — AI recommends, humans approve critical decisions.

3. How I Applied the Learning in the Certification

The certification assessment is scenario-based — which I found genuinely challenging and genuinely useful. Here is what helped me prepare and what I would tell anyone taking it next.

Scenario Type

What It Tests

My Approach

Client objection handling

Can you position BDC and Joule against a "we don't need to change" mindset?

Memorised the control tower analogy — ERPs are engines, BDC is the control tower. Never replace, always layer.

Persona-based positioning

Which message lands with which persona — CIO vs CFO vs COO?

Built a mental matrix: CIO = integration + security, CFO = ROI + reconciliation time, COO = supply chain speed

Technical architecture

How does zero-copy federation work? What is the Knowledge Graph?

Used real examples: Knowledge Graph maps PO to Supplier to Invoice — grounding AI in business context, preventing hallucinations

Responsible AI scenarios

What happens to client data? How is compliance maintained?

Led with ISO 42001, data sovereignty, human-in-the-loop — not features, but governance principles

The exam rewards consultative thinking, not product recall. The question is rarely "what does Joule do?" It is almost always "given this client situation, what would you recommend and why?" Thinking like a consultant — not a salesperson — is the key.

4. What I Gained Beyond the Certificate

Certifications give you a credential. Good certifications give you a new lens. This one gave me both.

  • Confidence in client conversations — I can now discuss AI architecture, data sovereignty, and agent capabilities without hesitation or vagueness.
  • A structured positioning framework — I know which message to lead with for each persona and which technical detail to deploy for each objection.
  • Real examples that land — not "SAP has AI agents" but "the Cash Management Agent saves your finance team 70% of manual reconciliation time — here is exactly how."
  • A sustainability angle I was not expecting — the Green Ledger and Sustainability Tower capabilities genuinely surprised me with their depth and maturity.
  • A new appreciation for data — the courses reinforced that AI is a data story first and a technology story second. That reframing has changed how I approach every client engagement.
  • A structured positioning framework — I know which message to lead with for each persona and which technical detail to deploy for each objection.
  • Real examples that land — not "SAP has AI agents" but "the Cash Management Agent saves your finance team 70% of manual reconciliation time — here is exactly how."
  • A sustainability angle I was not expecting — the Green Ledger and Sustainability Tower capabilities genuinely surprised me with their depth and maturity.
  • A new appreciation for data — the courses reinforced that AI is a data story first and a technology story second. That reframing has changed how I approach every client engagement.

The most valuable moment in the entire certification was a scenario-based roleplay where I had to respond to a sceptical client who said "our ERP works fine, why change it?" Every concept from all three courses came together in that one moment. That is when I knew the learning had actually landed.

Final Thought

If you are considering this certification — do it. Not just for the badge, but for the way it reshapes how you think about AI, data, and enterprise transformation.

And when you sit down with your next client who says "we are curious about AI but not sure where to start" — you will know exactly what to say.

Start with the data. Layer the intelligence. Let the Flywheel turn.

If this was useful, please like and share. Questions or your own experiences? Drop them in the comments below. Happy to connect with fellow SAP learners.

entry.title: SAP BTP AI best practices – Document grounding
entry.content:
AI conversations are only as good as the information behind them. And honestly, that’s where many projects get stuck.

In the next session of our SAP BTP AI best practices series, we’ll explore something incredibly practical: document grounding on SAP BTP.

Think of it as giving AI access to the knowledge your enterprise already owns; policies, manuals, product documentation, internal guides, and making that information usable through out-of-the-box RAG workflows.

During this webinar, we’ll walk through the architecture of SAP AI Core, highlight the capabilities that make document grounding work, and share a few real-world examples of how partners are already putting it into action.

The goal?  Help you make enterprise data accessible in a way that feels simple, scalable, and honestly… very powerful.

If you’re building AI solutions on SAP BTP, this is one session you’ll want on your calendar.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013266.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #EnterpriseAI #AIArchitecture #RAG #SAPAI

entry.title: Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenario Part - 3
entry.content:
In Part 1, I explained the overall setup of B2B Advisory. How the TPM foundation was structured. With the Company, Trading Partner, and Systems configuration in place, the next critical layer is the Agreement.

In Part 2, I explained the setup of the agreement. How the agreemetn can help to identigy the business process along with the trading partner identifer how that works.

In this final part I will explain how the standard flow works and what customization I did to fullfill my requriement (integration with custom EDI XML).

Information about the standard EDI Integration package

Package name : Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2 (can be found in discovery)

There are multiple option of the trigger available based on how the actual call trigger for example in this we have the iflow for AS2, AS2 MDN, IDoc, Process Direct and SOAP. All of them are exposed with endpoints which can be used as per the requirement. 

Other then these iflow we have several other Iflows which are being used to process data as per the EDI File type along with to execute the business process defined in the agreements.

As my interface is the inbound interface where from one of the SFTP file needs to pick and IDoc ORDERS.ORDERS05. To complete this required I have create

flow 1 : which pick the file from the SFTP and parse/create the required headers which are essential for processing in further step.

the pasring steps is being handled by the 2nd flow Step 1b - write Message to Message Queue which I have modifed with customized XSLT and header, which support my configuration done while creating agreement (identifier for receiver).

The names of the headers I choose are not random, because in next IFlow where agreement identification are being executed, it picked these identifiers from the headers only to create agreement plain id. I have already mentioned the attributes of agreement plain ID and below are the headers list for the same.

OrderField in Match StringScript VariableHeader SourceCustomized
1Adapter TypesndAdapterTypeSAP_COM_SND_Adapter_Typecustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
2Document StandardsndDocumentStandardSAP_EDI_Document_Standardcustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
3Message NamespacesndMessageNamespaceSAP_EDI_Message_Namespace 
4Message VersionsndMessageVersionSAP_EDI_Message_Versioncustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
5Sender IDsndSenderIdSAP_EDI_Sender_IDcustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
6Sender ID QualifiersndSenderIdQualifierSAP_EDI_Sender_ID_Qualifiercustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
7Sender Partner TypesndSenderPartnerTypeSAP_EDI_Sender_Partner_Type 
8Sender System IDsndSenderSystemIdSAP_EDI_GS_Sender_ID (later overwritten to "") 
9Sender System ID QualifiersndSenderSystemIdQualifierSAP_EDI_GS_Sender_ID_Qualifier 
10Receiver IDsndReceiverIdSAP_EDI_Receiver_IDcustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
11Receiver ID QualifiersndReceiverIdQualifierSAP_EDI_Receiver_ID_Qualifiercustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
12Receiver Partner TypesndReceiverPartnerTypeSAP_EDI_Receiver_Partner_Type 
13Receiver System IDsndReceiverSystemIdSAP_EDI_GS_Receiver_ID (later overwritten to "") 
14Receiver System ID QualifiersndReceiverSystemIdQualifierSAP_EDI_GS_Receiver_ID_Qualifier 
15Message TypesndMessageTypeSAP_EDI_Message_Typecustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)

Groovy analysis : In case you have come accross the script "callPDwithHeaderParameters.groovy" you might seen the line where "PidStringTemplate" is being created with certain variables which are driven by the header mentioned above with some additional placeholder "%s" which further being replaced with "sndSenderId" and "sndReceiverId".

// pidStrintTemplate
def PidStringTemplate = sndAdapterType + "-" + sndDocumentStandard + "-" + sndMessageNamespace + "-" + sndMessageVersion + "-%s-" + sndSenderIdQualifier + "-" + sndSenderPartnerType + "-" + sndSenderSystemId + "-" + sndSenderSystemIdQualifier + "-%s-" + sndReceiverIdQualifier + "-" + sndReceiverPartnerType + "-" + sndReceiverSystemId + "-" + sndReceiverSystemIdQualifier + "-" + sndMessageType;

// placing senderID and receiverID for placeholders %s
def defaultPid = String.format(PidStringTemplate, sndSenderId, sndReceiverId)

// creating parter ID plain string and md5 hash header
message.setHeader("ACTUAL_PARTNER_ID", partnerID);
message.setHeader("SAP_Partner_ID_MATCH_String", matchString);
message.setHeader("SAP_TPM_ACTIVITYPARTNER_ID", partnerID);

These are the complete steps which need to be performed for customized EDIFacts processing and what I did to achive my requriements.

 

Additional resource

Some times for partner based value mapping, we mainted the general data inside the transactions of an agreement, which can help during the dynamic field data mapping. to pick these values from agreement below code can be utilized. This code get the value from one of the header "ACTUAL_PARTNER_ID" which can be use to lookup inside the partnerDirectory data. All the general data we maintain under Activity paramters (agreement transaction) are stored under the "Binary parameter" with the ID "SAP_TPM_CustomActivityParams" in the form of JSon.

import com.sap.it.api.pd.PartnerDirectoryService;
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory;
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import groovy.json.*;
import com.sap.it.api.pd.BinaryData;

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def pdService = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService.class, null)
    if (pdService == null) throw new Exception("Partner Directory Service not available")
    def partnerID = message.getHeaders().get("ACTUAL_PARTNER_ID");

    if (partnerID != null) {
        // dynamic parameters for custom activity
		def customActivityParams = pdService.getParameter("SAP_TPM_CustomActivityParams", partnerID, BinaryData.class);
		if (customActivityParams != null){
			def customActivityParamsStr = new String(customActivityParams.getData());
			def customActivityParamsJson = new JsonSlurper().parseText(customActivityParamsStr);
			customActivityParamsJson.each{
				paramKey, paramValue -> message.setProperty(paramKey, paramValue);
			}
		}
    }
    return message
}

Apart from these sometime complex value map can be maintained under Manager Partner Directory (under Manage Stores) which generally can be without partner specific but may be based on the Counrty, plant etc. This can be access with below code.

import com.sap.it.api.pd.PartnerDirectoryService
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {
	
    // 1. Initialize Partner Directory Service
    def pdService = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService.class, null)
    if (pdService == null) throw new Exception("Partner Directory Service not available")

    // 2. Handle First PID (Suffix logic)
    // Assuming input PID comes from a header or property named 'Input_PID'
    String inputPid = message.getHeaders().get("PD_APID") ?: message.getProperties().get("PD_APID")
    
	if (inputPid) {
        String pid1 = inputPid + "_PD"
        String val1 = pdService.getParameter("SNDPRN", pid1, String.class)
		String val2 = pdService.getParameter("SNDPRT", pid1, String.class)
        message.setProperty("Partner_SNDPRN", val1 ?: "") // Empty if null
		message.setProperty("Partner_SNDPRT", val2 ?: "") // Empty if null
    }

    // 3. Handle Second PID (EDI_DC40_QA)
    String pid2 = message.getHeaders().get("SAP_ID") ?: message.getProperties().get("SAP_ID")
    
	// These can be changed based on the requirement and ID's which maintained uder Partner Directory
	def paramList = ["SNDPOR", "RCVPOR", "RCVPRT", "RCVPRN"]

    paramList.each { paramId ->
        String value = pdService.getParameter(paramId, pid2, String.class)
        // Store in property named 'Partner_SNDPRT', 'Partner_SNDPOR', etc.
        message.setProperty("Partner_" + paramId, value ?: "")
    }
    return message
}

 

Thanks

Aman Sharma

entry.title: From setup to value: Activating SAP Joule for Developers
entry.content:
AI is changing how developers build software.  Not in theory, in their daily workflow.

That’s exactly where SAP Joule comes in.

In our upcoming session, “From setup to value: Activating SAP Joule for Developers,” we’ll explore how generative AI is becoming part of the SAP development experience.  Think of it as moving from “AI sounds interesting” to “AI is actually helping me code and build faster.”

The thing is, activation is often the step that slows teams down.  What needs to be configured?  What are the prerequisites?  Where do you even begin?

We’ll walk through all of that.  Step by step.

Plus, you’ll see how Joule connects into the broader SAP Business Technology Platform environment and what partners should know when enabling it for development teams and customers.

Honestly, if you're building, integrating, or innovating on SAP solutions, this is one of those sessions that can save you a lot of guesswork.

Join us live and learn how to bring AI directly into the developer experience.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013283.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPAI #DeveloperExperience #EnterpriseAI #SAPCommunity

entry.title: Your Blueprint to Lead the Shift in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
entry.content:
The Utilities industry is moving fast toward cloud-ready architectures, customer-first operations, and intelligent,  AI-enabled processes. If you're an architect, business user , or consultant, you're at the center of this transformation.  

The journey begins with a thorough introduction to SAP S/4HANA Utilities, covering unique system capabilities, end-to-end industry processes, and customer service operations. From there, you will explore the strategic differences between SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA Utilities, learning to evaluate conversion options and apply proven transition methodologies using tools like SAP Readiness Check. 

The curriculum then guides you through essential system configuration, including master data modeling, device management with inventory and procurement processes, meter reading, and integration with other SAP components. Billing and invoicing are addressed in depth, from rate structure configuration to budget billing plans and exception handling. 

For the German energy market, specialized training covers market communication processes, including the cloud-based SAP Market Communication (MaCo) solution, Application Process Engine (APE), and the integration into SAP S/4HANA Utilities with a focus on regulatory compliance and automation. 

Finally, the curriculum prepares you to build future-proof architectures by keeping the SAP S/4HANA core clean through extensibility on SAP Business Technology Platform, and utilizing SAP Fiori for a modern, industry-specific user experience. 

Ready to go hands on? 

Build end-to-end skills with the refreshed course stack:  

  1. S4IUT is your overview and entry point to the SAP S/4HANA for Utilities. The course covers  the business processes , Fiori-based functions, AI-enabled optimization, asset management architecture, and cloud integrations: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/executing-business-processes-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  2. IUT21 focuses on master data and basic functions configuration: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-master-data-and-basic-functions-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  3. IUT22 deep-dives into device management configuration, from master data and inventory to installations, inspections, and meter reading: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-device-management-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  4. IUT23 covers billing and invoicing configuration, including rate structures, execution, exceptions, and budget billing: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-billing-and-invoicing-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  5. If you operate in the German energy market, IUT51 explores market communication (MaCo-Cloud) with the Application Process Engine (APE): https://learning.sap.com/courses/explore-sap-market-communication-for-utilities-in-the-german-energy-market 
  6. Round it out with the updated S4IUD gives you the strategic and technical insights to guide customers from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA with confidence: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/transitioning-to-sap-s-4hana-for-utilities 

Ready to lead your customers’ transition with clarity and impact? Start with S4IUT (or S4IUD), then progress through the updated stack to build execution and configuration expertise.

entry.title: DDCR™ — The Technology-Agnostic Engine That Eliminates Integration Sprawl in Enterprise Architecture
entry.content:
Update — 12/03/2026 - 16:03pm -  IA LLM Tested - DDCR works - The last update on this blog.

I have updated the text, refined the terminology, and added additional diagrams and images to explain the architecture in the most didactic and simple way possible — aiming to reach what I call “level 0 of abstraction.”

This update has absolutely nothing to do with criticizing or diminishing SAP in any way. On the contrary, I have deep gratitude and respect for the ecosystem where I have worked with SAP technologies as a professional expert for more than 17 years, and where I also published as a SAP Press E-Bite Author (Enterprise Messaging — 2021).

To clarify, I have worked with SAP products, not as an employee of SAP. Everything presented here reflects my personal work and independent research as an enterprise integration architect.

The ideas described in this article were developed independently and do not follow any specific vendor manuals, frameworks, or official architectural guidelines. The objective was simply to investigate and attempt to solve a problem that I have observed repeatedly in enterprise integration landscapes.

This article represents the personal opinion and architectural perspective of the creator of SDIA, GDCR, DDCR, and ODCP. It is not officially affiliated with, endorsed by, or associated with SAP or any other technology vendor mentioned in this document.

All vendor technologies referenced here are used purely as examples of platforms where the concepts were tested and validated.

In fact, the initial ideas that eventually evolved into this architecture were born while working with SAP technologies, particularly within SAP BTP Integration Suite (APIM and CPI) in Feb 2026.

I would also like to thank the SAP BTP infrastructure team, because during the experimentation and validation phases I likely sent more than 800,000 - zero erros - messages through SAP BTP Trial (APIM and CPI) while testing the DDCR engine and the broader SDIA architecture and also SAP CPI as backend.

Thank you very much.

 
 

Introduction

In many enterprise integration landscapes, complexity does not arrive all at once. It grows silently.

A new API is created for one business unit. Another package is added for a second system. A new team introduces its own routing convention. Then another gateway policy appears. Then a different platform. Then a workaround. Then another technical user. Then more duplicated logic.

Over time, what should have been a coherent integration architecture becomes a fragmented landscape of flows, policies, scripts, mappings, and endpoint decisions spread across multiple tools and teams.

That is the problem of integration sprawl endless ploriferation.

In the API Management layer, it appears as API proxy sprawl — often a 1:1 relationship between proxies and backends.

In the orchestration layer, it becomes package or orchestration-object sprawl.

At the same time, organizations face technical user sprawl across both API Management and orchestration platforms.

The result is simple: sprawl everywhere — continuously growing, poorly organized, and increasingly difficult to govern.

With the rapid introduction of AI and LLM-based services, the challenge becomes even greater. The number of integrations, endpoints, and routing scenarios will only continue to expand, making it even harder for enterprises to maintain control over their integration landscape.

I believe there is a way to address this.

What I am proposing is an architectural approach that I am sharing freely for anyone — any developer, any architect, any company, anything — to explore and adopt.

This approach does not replace existing technical platforms or silos. Instead, it sits above them, introducing a semantic domain layer end to end in all objets names and developments, that helps organize whole enterprise architecture landscape

Understanding this model requires a certain level of architectural abstraction beyond vendor manuals, but once understood, it offers a different way of thinking about enterprise integration.

This is where SDIA, with GDCR™ and DDCR™ for API Management platforms, or DDCR™ alone for other technologies, comes into play.

 

What is SDIA?

SDIA stands for Semantic Domain Integration Architecture.

It is not a product. Not a framework. Not a library. Not a vendor offering.

If you are looking for something to install, buy, or subscribe to — you are looking in the wrong direction.

It is an architectural way of thinking about integration in which routing, control, name convention and organization are driven by domain meaning rather than technical fragmentation.

The diagram illustrates the core idea behind SDIA.

A client request expresses a semantic intent, for example:

/sales/orders/create/salesforce

This request first enters the GDCR semantic API facade, where the domain meaning of the request is interpreted.

The DDCR resolver then converts this semantic intent into a deterministic routing key and resolves the correct backend route through metadata.

Finally, the request reaches the ODCP orchestration layer, where the integration flow is executed and connected to enterprise systems such as SAP, databases, or data lakes.

In simple terms:

Domain intent → deterministic resolution (DNS INTERNET) → orchestration execution

The important point is that the domain meaning controls the routing, while the underlying technologies remain interchangeable.

So in simple terms:

https://community.sap.com/ → DNS → 10.983.049.33
/sales/... → DDCR domain sales routing → any backend /sales/...

Just like DNS resolves community.sap.com to an IP address, SDIA uses DDCR to resolve a semantic domain path like /sales/... into the correct backend route for the Sales domain, independently of the underlying technology.

A domain can represent business capabilities, products, countries, cities, hospitals, IoT ecosystems, LLM services, or any other semantic boundary that defines how systems should interact.

This means the entire integration chain follows the same semantic boundary.

Sales Domain Example

Sales domain intent (HTTP request)

  • Sales API Proxy

  • Sales CPI Package

  • Sales iFlow= organized enterprise integration architecture end-to-end

Example:

  • /sales/orders/create/salesforce

Finance Domain Example

Finance domain intent (HTTP request)

  • Finance API Proxy

  • Finance CPI Package

  • Finance iFlow = organized finance integration landscape

Example:

  • /finance/invoices/create/s4hana

Principle

  • Sales facade + Sales + Sales + Sales = Organized architecture
  • Finance facade + Finance + Finance + Finance = Organized architecture

The domain remains stable. The domain never lies.The technology remains interchangeable.

SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture) is a high-level enterprise integration architecture that operates above specific technologies and vendor implementations. Rather than organizing integration landscapes around technical systems or tools, SDIA centers architecture on semantic business domains, allowing routing, governance, and orchestration to remain stable even as underlying platforms evolve.

It can be described as an invisible architectural layer, because it does not bind itself to any particular gateway, middleware, or orchestration engine. Instead, it defines the semantic rules, naming conventions, and routing logic that guide how technologies interact within the integration ecosystem.

Within SDIA, technologies such as API gateways, event brokers, orchestration engines, and messaging platforms become execution mechanisms, while architectural decisions remain defined at the semantic domain level.

In this sense, DDCR provides a domain-centric resolution mechanism for enterprise integration, similar to how DNS provides resolution for the internet.

Instead of scattering routing logic across conditional policies, scripts, and platform-specific configurations, routing decisions become deterministic lookups derived from semantic intent.

To compose this model, SDIA builds upon several well-known architectural patterns from the integration literature, including:

  • Enterprise Integration Patterns (EIP)

  • Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)

  • Saga Pattern for distributed transactions

  • Transactional Outbox Pattern (OQS)

  • API Gateway Pattern

  • Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) architectural concepts

  • CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)

Rather than replacing these patterns, SDIA organizes and contextualizes them within a domain-centric architectural model, enabling consistent governance and deterministic routing across heterogeneous integration platforms end to end.

Instead of asking:

  • Which gateway product is being used?

  • Which vendor-specific policy should be configured?

  • Which endpoint is hardcoded in this flow?

  • Which package owns this route?

SDIA asks a different question:

What is the domain intent of this request?

Once the domain intent is clear, the architecture organizes itself around that meaning rather than around scattered technical artifacts.

In simple terms, SDIA provides end-to-end semantic domain organization, structured through three components:

SDIA™ ( Semantic Domain Integration Architecture)
├─ GDCR™(governance + control architecture — API Management)
│
├─ DDCR™(deterministic routing engine — (Anything)
│
└─ ODCP™(Semantic Domain Topology of Naming Convention over any objects regards the integration in the cloud or  on-premise middleware layer for any vendor)

│
└─ Technology background - What ever technology but in front SDIA.

Together they create a domain-centered integration architecture across the entire landscape.

This blog focuses specifically on the DDCR engine, a deterministic semantic routing mechanism based on mathematical principles. In testing, the engine has never failed across approximately ~2,067,873 messages, implemented in five different programming languages and validated across eight technical platforms.

Tools & Technologies Validated

Languages

  • JavaScript (SAP BTP APIM — Target Endpoint PreFlow)
  • Lua (Kong Gateway — custom plugin)
  • Java (Netflix Zuul — DdcrPhantomFilter route filter)
  • Python (AWS Lambda + IoT MQTT publisher)
  • C# (Azure API Management — policy inbound)

API Gateways & APIM

  • SAP BTP API Management
  • Kong Gateway (standalone)
  • Kong Gateway on Kubernetes
  • Netflix Zuul (Spring Cloud, Java)
  • AWS API Gateway + Lambda
  • Azure API Management

Orchestration & Backend

  • SAP BTP Integration Suite (CPI)

KVM / Metadata Stores

  • SAP KVM (native BTP)
  • Redis (standalone local)
  • Redis on Kubernetes (Docker Desktop)
  • In-memory HashMap (Java static)

Infrastructure & Containers

  • Kubernetes (Docker Desktop, namespace sdia)
  • Istio Service Mesh
  • Docker (Redis, Kong, Node-RED, Mosquitto)

IoT Stack

  • Mosquitto MQTT Broker
  • Node-RED
  • Python MQTT publisher (42 sensors, 4 environments)

DDCR defines the semantic control logic that brings order, consistency, and determinism to enterprise integration.

 

What is DDCR?

Yes, the name can sound confusing at first. In practice, DDCR is simply the routing logic I created based on deterministic semantics and mathematical principles.

Its like java, why java ? why linux ? The name from the creator, so I choose DDCR

In SAP API Management, it is implemented as a JavaScript policy placed in the Target Endpoint PreFlow. That’s it. Nothing more.

In other platforms the implementation changes, but the logic remains the same.

  • In SAP BTP API Management → JavaScript policy

  • In Kong → Lua plugin

  • In Azure API Management → C# inbound policy

  • In AWS → Lambda authorizer

  • In Netflix Zuul → Java route filter

The language changes.The engine does not.

  • You do not replace your SAP API Management platform.
  • You do not change your infrastructure.
  • You do not lose money.
  • You do not lose knowlege
  • You do not lose your job or your project.

Just inject the engine — a simple script together with the Key Value Map (KVM) policy — and the domain takes control of the routing logic.

Check the image below, that's it, you see DDCR alive into your API Proxy, no mistery, first the KVM to bring the strings with KEY and the JavaScript Policy process all steps, without any problem and the result is always the same as 4+5 =9 neve fails, impossible.

If fails it's guard fast-fail m2m in less than ~0.1ms, than what is the problem ?

Possible a wrong string format in the kvm or the URL intention is wrong.

One script. Any vendor. Any language.

DDCR is the deterministic routing engine that operates inside the SDIA architecture model.

As the creator of the DDCR (Domain Driven Centric Router) logic, I am currently preparing the formal mathematical documentation of its deterministic routing model for academic submission to institutions such as IEEE and other research bodies, wish me luck this might be very good for all community in soon future.

 

DDCR inside GDCR

Where it started — and why it left.

In 7th of February 2026, this development began inside SAP — APIM and CPI, under the names DCRP and PDCP. The architecture was already working. The results were already real.

 

 

But the environment was not ready for it.

Gatekeepers who see only manuals, buttons, and instructions have a natural defense mechanism against things that do not fit inside a checkbox. That is not a criticism — it is a structural reality of large organizations. Innovation that cannot be approved by committee tends to get delayed, diluted, or quietly buried.

So I made a decision: stop explaining to people who are not ready to listen, and start building proof that cannot be ignored.

Everything moved outside. No distractions. No approvals. No politics.

Before you touch any code, read the documents. All four of them. This is not optional — it is the prerequisite. The architecture has a specific vocabulary, a specific invariant, and a specific logic. Without that foundation, the code will look like a simple routing script and you will miss everything that matters.

If you have done that — and if you have real APIM experience — the rest takes about 10 minutes.

Not because it is simple. Because it is precise.

Direct link for all objects regards SAP BTP Integration Suite APIM and CPI

https://github.com/rhviana/sdia/tree/main/gdcr-proven/gdcr-drcp-sap-api

 

As you can see in the GitHub repository, all the required artifacts are available. You only need to read the material, understand the concepts, download the files, and test them.

If it is not possible to test this in your company environment, you can simply create a SAP BTP Trial account with API Management and CPI, exactly as I did for this experiment.

Inside the repository you will find:

  • 4 API proxies for four domains (Sales, Finance, Procurement, and Logistics) — plug-and-play deployment

  • 4 CPI packages containing 42 iFlows — plug-and-play deployment

  • A Postman collection with everything prepared — you only need to change the host, username, and password

  • Newman scripts ready for load testing (be careful with the 200k test runs — that means 200,000 messages)

  • Example JSON and XML payloads with dummy data prepared for Newman execution

Sample of KVM git:

kvm name: cpipackage-nx.sales.o2c.integrations - One API Proxy for SALES process O2C - N:1 - KVM abaixo

kvm.idinterface
dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http,dcrpordersusalesforceemeaid02:http,dcrpcustomerssshopifyid03:http,dcrppaymentsnstripeid04:http,dcrporderscmicrosoftid05:cxf,dcrpdeliveriestfedexid06:http,dcrpcustomersss4hanaid07:cxf,dcrppaymentsns4hanaid08:cxf,dcrpinvoicescquickbooksid09:cxf,dcrpinvoicescs4hanaid10:cxf,dcrpdeliveriests4hanaid11:cxf,dcrpreturnscshopifyid12:http

kvm.sapprocess - SAP SD & O2C - Sales Process

kvm.package - nx.sales.o2c.integrations (SAP CPI Package for Sales - Process O2C where all iflows regards this domain are centralized, not from technical silos or fragmented decentralized name convention)

If I have another process within Sales, I simply create another proxy such as SALES process XXX — N:1, and then create the corresponding key strings. There is no mystery in that.

The most interesting proxy is:

gdcr-kvm-idinterface-string-control-plane.zip

I created this specifically for SAP API Management. It exposes a small HTML + JavaScript UI that allows you to create, update, and delete KVM entries safely, helping to avoid human errors when managing the routing metadata.

I will explain this UI in the next section.

 Control Painel – Add string KVM

The self-service configuration UI (HTML + JavaScript) for zero-error KVM metadata deployment

API Management API-PROXY

  • gdcr-kvm-idinterface-string-control-plane

API Management API-PROXY – Flow:

 

Add first CORS first because your HTML+JavaScript UI (running in the browser) calls this proxy to write entries into the KVM, and the browser needs valid CORS headers already on the preflight OPTIONS request.

Short step‑by‑step of the policy flow:

  1. p-cors
    Handles CORS preflight and adds Access-Control-Allow-* headers so the browser is allowed to call this API from your HTML/JS UI.
  2. p-extractNam…
    Extracts parameters from path/query/body (domain, entity, action, vendor, etc.) and stores them in variables the script will use.
  3. p-getkvmidst…
    Reads the current KVM entry for this domain/package (for example the kvm.idinterface string). If nothing exists yet, it returns empty.
  4. jsc-build-kv…
    In JavaScript you normalize the action (create→c, sync→s), build the compact key dcrp<entity><actionCode><vendor><id>:<adapter>, check for duplicates, and create the updated KVM string.
  5. p-injectStri…
    Writes the new string back to the KVM, updating the control‑plane metadata without manual editing.
  6. p-cors-respo…
    Ensures CORS headers are also present on the final response so the browser accepts the reply from the API.

So: proxy and engine stay static, the KVM metadata is updated safely via this UI flow, and CORS at the beginning/end makes the browser happy.

Sample of execution:

KVM sample string:

 

 

 

This UI is provided in the appendix. It is a small HTML + JavaScript tool that interacts with SAP API Management to manage KVM entries.

A JavaScript policy in the ProxyEndpoint PreFlow checks if a value already exists in the KVM string. If it exists, nothing changes; if not, the value is appended. Deleting an entry removes it automatically from the KVM string.

This avoids manual KVM edits and reduces configuration errors.

A prototype LLM assistant to generate KVM strings from routing intents is being developed, but it is out of scope for this document.

JavaScript (frontend snippet):

js

const apiUrl =

  "<add your APIM management address with httppath from the API>" +

  encodeURIComponent(kvmNameFromField);

// Basic Auth

const user = "<user>";

const pass = "<pass>";

Important: before using the HTML page, you must update the API endpoint parameters and authentication credentials in the JavaScript file to match your own SAP API Management environment.

SAP CPI I also created but I could not find a clear way to create the package and the Iflow, but there is also UI static to generate for you in 5 seconds:

Both name convention are https://github.com/rhviana/sdia/tree/main/gdcr-proven/gdcr-drcp-sap-api/kvm

However, DDCR is also powerful because it can be used independently.

It does not need to exist only inside GDCR.

It can be applied in isolation in any environment that needs semantic routing and deterministic destination resolution.

For example, DDCR can be implemented in:

  • API Management platforms ( Target End-Point — PREFLOW) - tested
  • Kubernetes ingress layers - tested
  • service meshes - tested
  • reverse proxies - tested
  • zero-trust gateways - tested
  • event brokers - tested
  • IoT gateways - tested
  • LLM gateways - tested. 12/03/2026 - 15:50pm

I just tested two real scenarios and it worked flawlessly.
One single proxy. One engine. Zero code changes.

Now remember the SDIA - what is the domain intent of this request ? LLM

The domain ( /llm/ ) is the entry gate.
DDCR resolves everything else. Semantically. Deterministically. Universally.

Scenario 1 — AI-Powered Order Risk Analysis

📦SAP S/4HANA creates order
⬇️
https://<apim-host>/llm/fin/invoice/create/sap
🔀SAP APIM
KVM Key - gdcr.fin.invoice.c.sap.id04:http
DDCR routes https://<sapcpi>/gdcr/fin/invoice/c/id04
⬇️⚙️SAP CPI
⬇️🤖Groq AI (LLaMA)
⬇️
✅"High risk order — block or approve"

Scenario 2 — Industrial IoT + AI

🌡️ Factory sensor fires — temp=92, pressure=1200
⬇️
https://<apim-host>/llm/iot/sensor/analyze/openai
🔀SAP APIM
KVM Key - gdcr.ai.sensor.p.openai.id01:http
DDCR routes https://<sapcpi>/gdcr/ai/sensor/p/id01
⬇️⚙️SAP CPI
⬇️🤖Groq AI (LLaMA)
⬇️🔧"Critical risk — trigger maintenance in 2 hours"

The AI era is now under control.

Once you start implementing the architecture I am proposing — SDIA — the chaos ends.

I further optimized the key lookup to achieve O(1) binary access, reducing latency from 4 ms to 0.9 ~1.6 ms.
The internal implementation of this optimization will not be released, which is why the public key format uses dot-separated segments (.).

The public implementation demonstrates the deterministic routing model.
The private optimization only improves runtime efficiency and does not change the architectural principles of DDCR.

 

  • middleware runtimes
  • serverless routing layers - tested

This is one of its strongest characteristics:

DDCR is technology-agnostic and platform-agnostic.

It is not locked to a vendor. It is not locked to a product. It is not locked to a gateway.

It can run anywhere a request can be intercepted and routed.

DDCR, in turn, acts as the deterministic routing engine, responsible for resolving the semantic intent of a request and directing it to the correct destination based on metadata.

What is metadata here?

Anything capable of storing and returning a string.

In SAP BTP API Management it is the KVM policy.

In Kong, a Redis key.

In AWS, DynamoDB or Parameter Store.

In Kubernetes, a ConfigMap.

But it can also be a plain text file, an XML document, a JSON file, a REST API, a SOAP webservice, a database table, a spreadsheet — anything that holds a key and returns a value on demand.

The engine does not care what stores the key. It only cares that the key resolves deterministically.

 

 Before the three examples below, I would like to give you an internal view of how the DDCR engine works.

I am the sole enterprise integration architect, engineer, SAP BTP expert, SAP Presse Author, and independent researcher responsible for the design, formalization, documentation, and implementation of DDCR and the broader Semantic Domain Integration Architecture (SDIA).

Brazilian. Independent. No budget. No team. No company behind it.

The core architectural concepts were conceived, formalized, documented, and validated across 8 platforms in 32 days — using only trial accounts from each vendor, a personal notebook, and the kind of determination that only comes from seeing the same problem destroy every enterprise integration landscape you have ever worked in.

No marketing. No peers. No support. Just nights over nights over nights.

Where I see chaos, I give order.

The result is a complete architectural definition with 3 pillars, validated with ~2,067,873 requests, zero failures, across 5 programming languages and every major integration layer that exists today.

This is not a side project. This is the answer to a problem the industry has been ignoring for 20 years.

I have already received direct feedback via linkedin from one of the most respected independent thought leaders in enterprise integration — someone whose published contributions to the field date back to 2003 and remain reference material for architects worldwide today.

The response came within one hour after my message.

Comming back to DDCR:

It operates based on deterministic domain semantics — essentially applied mathematics.

The principle behind it is completely technology-agnostic, just like mathematics.

2 + 2 = 4 and 5 + 5 = 10 or 16–6 = 10 anywhere in the world — regardless of the technology, vendor, platform, country, company, hardware, infrastructure, programming language, language ( English, Portuguese, Russian, Chinese, Japonese) all the same result.

Because the logic is mathematical, the engine behaves consistently across any technology stack.

As you read the following examples, please pay close attention to one thing above all:

the intention of the person making the request.

The bookstore analogy: the easiest way to understand the DDCR enginne

Imagine a person walking into a bookstore.

A student walks into a library and approaches the librarian.

→ “Good morning, I’d like to borrow a mathematics book.”

His intent: /library/borrow/books/mathematics

The librarian has worked there for 50 years. Listens. Consults a notebook with a key

booksmathematicg01

Responds:

→”Of course — your book is in the mathematics section, first floor.”

 

Target Result: /books/section/mathematics/firstfloor

Student — “Thank you , Im going there now, section, mathematics at firsfloor”

 

How the DDCR Engine Works - Remember DDCR it's just nice name that's it

As I explained earlier, DDCR is only the logic. In the case of SAP API Management, it is implemented as JavaScript, and the code I used for the proof of concept (POC) is already included inside the configured proxies available on GitHub.

It is impossible for me to create production keys and a production-ready JavaScript implementation without knowing the specific landscape and active domains of your organization.

The code i drop to the end of blog, I could not use the spoiller it's too large.

So in the SAP APIM Design Runtime 

 

key value mapping returns this texts:

dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http,dcrpordersusalesforceemeaid02:http,dcrpcustomerssshopifyid03:http,dcrppaymentsnstripeid04:http,dcrporderscmicrosoftid05:cxf,dcrpdeliveriestfedexid06:http,dcrpcustomersss4hanaid07:cxf,dcrppaymentsns4hanaid08:cxf,dcrpinvoicescquickbooksid09:cxf,dcrpinvoicescs4hanaid10:cxf,dcrpdeliveriests4hanaid11:cxf,dcrpreturnscshopifyid12:http

The next step the javascript ( DDCR ) reads from the runtime the http intent url

/sales/orders/create/salesforce --- match --- runtime -- /orders/create/salesforce  -- dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http -- target http/dcrp/orders/s/id01 - the code ignores the sender, he wants the domain only.

At its core, DDCR follows a 7-stage deterministic routing pipeline. Zero regex. Zero hardcode. Zero split. Pure mathematics.

Stage 1 — Domain Inference The engine intercepts the incoming request and parses the semantic URL using manual indexOf — no regex, no split.

Stage 2 — Policy Execution AuthZ and Zero Trust boundary enforcement. The request identity is validated before any routing logic executes.

Stage 3 — Route Guard (Fast Fail — M2M) If the semantic URL is malformed or the intent is invalid, the engine rejects immediately at ~0.1ms. The backend never receives an invalid request.

Stage 4 — Normalize The action verb is canonicalized. 241 variants map to 15 codes.

Stage 5 — Build KVM Key The engine constructs the deterministic metadata key.

[prefix][entity][actionCode][vendor][flowId]:[adapter]

dcrp · orders · c · Salesforce · id01 : http

→ dcrporderscSalesforceid01:http

Stage 6 — Resolve O(1) lookup against the KVM store. Compatible with Redis, DynamoDB, HashMap, Kubernetes ConfigMap, PostgreSQL, etcd — any key-value store.

/sales/orders/create/salesforce
→ dcrporderscSalesforceid01:http
→ https://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01

Stage 7 — Dispatch The request is forwarded to the resolved backend. The consumer URL never changes — ever.

Consumer: POST /sales/<entity>/<action>/<target> ← permanent
Backend: https://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01 ← resolved

In one sentence:

The engine converts semantic intent into a deterministic routing key, resolves it via O(1) KVM lookup, and dispatches — while fast-failing any invalid intent before it reaches the backend.

Intent → Inference → Policy → Guard → Normalize → Key → Resolve → Dispatch

Conclusion

DDCR is not a framework. Not a library. Not a vendor product. It is a mathematical invariant — a governing principle that emerges from the nature of enterprise integration itself.

As SDIA is not product, no, it's not a product it's a high abstract enterprise integration architecture based on semantic domains, in the background vendors, groovy script, jdbc, xslt, java script, soap, rest bla bla bla bla but what is the domain intention ? This is end of sprawl worldwide.

The Problem — API Sprawl (today)

One proxy per backend. One package per vendor. This is the current reality in most enterprises:

 

With SDIA + GDCR — 4 addresses. Forever.

 
1 proxy. 1 package. 1 OpenAPI spec. Forever.
Add 1 vendor = 1 metadata entry. 30 seconds. Zero deploy. Zero new proxy. Zero new spec.

In SAP APIM — before vs after

The proof is not theoetical. It is empirical.

~2,067,873 requests. Zero routing failures. Sub-4ms latency. 99.99% uptime. Validated across 8 platforms, 5 programming languages, and every major integration layer — APIM, orchestration, Kubernetes, service mesh, IoT edge, and AI gateway — under constrained conditions, using free-tier trial accounts, local Docker environments, and community editions. No production budget. No team. No vendor support.

One engineer. One invariant. One result.

The four publications establishing prior art are registered on Zenodo under open DOIs, protected under USPTO Trademark Application Serial , and indexed in the IP.com Prior Art Database:

SDIA Governing architecture umbrella 10.5281/zenodo.18877636

IP — IPCOM000277630D

GDCR Semantic gateway facade 10.5281/zenodo.18836272

IP — IPCOM000277632D

DDCR Deterministic routing engine 10.5281/zenodo.18864833

IP — IPCOM000277633D

ODCP Orchestration naming convention 10.5281/zenodo.18876594

IP — IPCOM000277631D

Github — Free with all details, use, download, implement all codes everything is there, still under construction in some areas:

https://github.com/rhviana/sdia

All publications are released under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).

The only requirement of the CC BY 4.0 license is proper attribution to the original author when the work is publicly referenced or used.

The architecture is intentionally open and vendor-neutral. Any engineer, organization, or system in the world is free to study, implement, extend, and build upon it — including for commercial purposes.

No fees, royalties, or permissions are required, no money required, is FOR FREE ! Just read and plugin and play on SAP APIM and CPI test it, you will see reduction of SPRAWL in 90%.

This was a deliberate choice. Not a default.

I made this free because integration sprawl is a problem that affects every engineer, every company, and every enterprise on the planet. The solution should belong to everyone — not to a vendor, not to a product, not to a subscription.

This is dedicated to The Commander — the discipline, the sleepless nights, the determination to build something real without asking for permission.

The engine is open. The authorship is not. It is mine. Forever.

I don’t want anything in return — no recognition, no status, nothing, as DDCR zero fail.
If people apply it and it helps them, that’s enough for me.
I’m already moving forward toward the next challenges I have in mind.

Help ? Send me message, I replay when I have time, I'm doing last documentations and github.

The domain never lies.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana, March 2026 https://orcid.org/0009-0009-9549-5862

Phantom JavaScript language with DDCR logic:

/**
 * ============================================================================
 * GDCR Routing Engine – Phantom v12 (PRODUCTION READY)
 * ============================================================================
 * Author:    Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana
 * Email:     rhviana@gmail.com
 * License:   CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)
 * DOI:       https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18619641
 * GitHub:    https://github.com/rhviana/gdcr
 *
 * Zero regex | Zero hardcode | Zero split | O(1) lookup after warm cache
 * Compatible: SAP BTP APIM (Rhino) | Apigee | Kong (with adapter)
 * ============================================================================
 *
 * KVM key format:  dcrp<process><entity><actioncode><vendor>id<digits>
 * KVM value format: <adapter>
 * Example:
 *   Key:   dcrpfinanceorderscsalesforceid01
 *   Value: http
 *
 * Path format: /<entity>/<action>/<vendor>[/<id>[/<extra>...]]
 * ============================================================================
 */

// ── Canonical Action Map — Phantom v12 ──────────────────────────────────────
// 241 action variants → 15 canonical single-character codes
// Author: Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana
// DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492

var GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP = {

  // ── c: CREATE (27 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "create":"c",    "created":"c",    "post":"c",
  "insert":"c",    "add":"c",        "new":"c",
  "submit":"c",    "register":"c",   "registered":"c",
  "provision":"c", "provisioning":"c","onboard":"c",
  "onboarding":"c","publish":"c",    "publishing":"c",
  "generate":"c",  "build":"c",      "built":"c",
  "open":"c",      "opening":"c",    "setup":"c",
  "initialize":"c","initializing":"c","initialized":"c",
  "draft":"c",

  // ── r: READ (25 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "read":"r",      "get":"r",        "fetch":"r",
  "retrieve":"r",  "view":"r",       "viewed":"r",
  "list":"r",      "listing":"r",    "listed":"r",
  "show":"r",      "showing":"r",    "shown":"r",
  "search":"r",    "searching":"r",  "searched":"r",
  "find":"r",      "finding":"r",    "found":"r",
  "query":"r",     "querying":"r",   "queried":"r",
  "lookup":"r",    "check":"r",      "inspect":"r",

  // ── u: UPDATE (27 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "update":"u",    "updating":"u",   "updated":"u",
  "put":"u",       "patch":"u",      "patching":"u",
  "modify":"u",    "modifying":"u",  "modified":"u",
  "change":"u",    "changing":"u",   "changed":"u",
  "edit":"u",      "editing":"u",    "edited":"u",
  "revise":"u",    "revising":"u",   "revised":"u",
  "amend":"u",     "amending":"u",   "amended":"u",
  "replace":"u",   "replacing":"u",  "replaced":"u",
  "upsert":"u",

  // ── d: DELETE (20 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "delete":"d",    "deleting":"d",   "deleted":"d",
  "remove":"d",    "removing":"d",   "removed":"d",
  "cancel":"d",    "cancelling":"d", "cancelled":"d",
  "terminate":"d", "terminating":"d","terminated":"d",
  "destroy":"d",   "destroying":"d", "destroyed":"d",
  "deactivate":"d","purge":"d",      "purging":"d",
  "purged":"d",    "discard":"d",

  // ── s: SYNC (18 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "sync":"s",         "syncing":"s",       "synced":"s",
  "synchronize":"s",  "synchronizing":"s", "synchronized":"s",
  "replicate":"s",    "replicating":"s",   "replicated":"s",
  "mirror":"s",       "mirroring":"s",     "mirrored":"s",
  "refresh":"s",      "refreshing":"s",    "refreshed":"s",
  "reconcile":"s",    "harmonize":"s",     "align":"s",

  // ── a: APPROVE (18 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "approve":"a",    "approving":"a",   "approved":"a",
  "authorize":"a",  "authorizing":"a", "authorized":"a",
  "accept":"a",     "accepting":"a",   "accepted":"a",
  "validate":"a",   "validating":"a",  "validated":"a",
  "confirm":"a",    "confirming":"a",  "confirmed":"a",
  "endorse":"a",    "certify":"a",     "signoff":"a",

  // ── n: NOTIFY (18 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "notify":"n",     "notifying":"n",   "notified":"n",
  "alert":"n",      "alerting":"n",    "alerted":"n",
  "inform":"n",     "informing":"n",   "informed":"n",
  "announce":"n",   "announcing":"n",  "announced":"n",
  "broadcast":"n",  "broadcasting":"n","broadcasted":"n",
  "emit":"n",       "trigger":"n",     "signal":"n",

  // ── t: TRANSFER (16 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────
  "transfer":"t",   "transferring":"t","transferred":"t",
  "send":"t",       "sending":"t",     "sent":"t",
  "move":"t",       "moving":"t",      "moved":"t",
  "migrate":"t",    "migrating":"t",   "migrated":"t",
  "forward":"t",    "forwarding":"t",  "relay":"t",
  "handoff":"t",

  // ── e: ENABLE (12 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "enable":"e",     "enabling":"e",    "enabled":"e",
  "activate":"e",   "activating":"e",  "activated":"e",
  "start":"e",      "starting":"e",    "started":"e",
  "resume":"e",     "resuming":"e",    "resumed":"e",

  // ── b: DISABLE (12 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "disable":"b",    "disabling":"b",   "disabled":"b",
  "deactivate":"b", "deactivating":"b","deactivated":"b",
  "stop":"b",       "stopping":"b",    "stopped":"b",
  "pause":"b",      "pausing":"b",     "paused":"b",

  // ── v: ARCHIVE (10 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "archive":"v",    "archiving":"v",   "archived":"v",
  "store":"v",      "storing":"v",     "stored":"v",
  "backup":"v",     "backing":"v",     "backed":"v",
  "retain":"v",

  // ── w: RESTORE (10 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "restore":"w",    "restoring":"w",   "restored":"w",
  "unarchive":"w",  "recover":"w",     "recovering":"w",
  "recovered":"w",  "rollback":"w",    "revert":"w",
  "reinstate":"w",

  // ── x: AUDIT (12 variants) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  "audit":"x",      "auditing":"x",    "audited":"x",
  "log":"x",        "logging":"x",     "logged":"x",
  "trace":"x",      "tracing":"x",     "traced":"x",
  "track":"x",      "tracking":"x",    "tracked":"x",

  // ── z: EXECUTE (12 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "execute":"z",    "executing":"z",   "executed":"z",
  "run":"z",        "running":"z",     "ran":"z",
  "process":"z",    "processing":"z",  "processed":"z",
  "compute":"z",    "computing":"z",   "computed":"z",

  // ── f: FLOW/ROUTE (10 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────
  "flow":"f",       "flowing":"f",     "flowed":"f",
  "route":"f",      "routing":"f",     "routed":"f",
  "dispatch":"f",   "dispatching":"f", "dispatched":"f",
  "pipeline":"f"

};

// Validation
var entries = Object.keys(GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP);
var codes = {};
entries.forEach(function(k) {
  var c = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[k];
  codes[c] = (codes[c] || 0) + 1;
});
// Total: 241 variants → 15 canonical codes
// ── Worker-scoped caches (survive across requests on same worker) ────────────
var _kvmCacheRaw  = null;   // raw KVM string last seen
var _kvmCacheMap  = null;   // parsed map: {lookupKey -> entry}
var _cpiHostRaw   = null;   // raw host last seen
var _cpiHostNorm  = null;   // normalized host (trailing slash removed)

// ── Char code constants (zero string allocation on comparison) ───────────────
var CC_SLASH = 47;   // '/'
var CC_UNDER = 95;   // '_'
var CC_0     = 48;   // '0'
var CC_9     = 57;   // '9'
var CC_A_LOW = 97;   // 'a'
var CC_Z_LOW = 122;  // 'z'

// ============================================================================
// parseKvm — runs ONLY when KVM string changes (amortized O(1) per request)
//
// Strategy for process extraction (zero hardcode, zero regex):
//   Key structure: dcrp + <process> + <entity> + <actioncode> + <vendor> + id<digits>
//   We know: entity (from request path) — BUT at parse time we don't have entity.
//   Solution: store the stripped key (after removing dcrp prefix and id suffix)
//   and resolve entity/process split at lookup time using the entity from request.
//   This keeps parse O(n) over KVM entries and lookup O(k) over map keys (k<=30).
// ============================================================================
function parseKvm(kvmString) {
  var map = {};
  var len = kvmString.length;
  var start = 0;

  while (start < len) {

    // ── Find entry boundaries ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    var comma = kvmString.indexOf(',', start);
    if (comma === -1) comma = len;

    var colon = kvmString.indexOf(':', start);
    if (colon === -1 || colon > comma) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyRaw  = kvmString.substring(start, colon);
    var adapter = kvmString.substring(colon + 1, comma);

    if (!keyRaw || !adapter) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyLow = keyRaw.toLowerCase();

    // ── Extract numeric ID from end of key (e.g. "id01" → "01") ──────────
    var idVal = '';
    var idPos = keyLow.lastIndexOf('id');
    if (idPos !== -1 && idPos < keyLow.length - 2) {
      var dStr = '';
      for (var di = idPos + 2; di < keyLow.length; di++) {
        var dc = keyLow.charCodeAt(di);
        if (dc >= CC_0 && dc <= CC_9) dStr += keyLow.charAt(di);
        else break;
      }
      if (dStr) idVal = dStr;
    }

    // ── Strip 'dcrp' prefix and 'id<digits>' suffix ───────────────────────
    // Result: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
    var stripped = keyLow.substring(4); // remove leading 'dcrp'
    if (idVal) {
      // remove trailing 'id' + digits
      stripped = stripped.substring(0, stripped.length - (2 + idVal.length));
    }

    // ── Store entry indexed by stripped key ───────────────────────────────
    // process is resolved at lookup time (we need entity from request path)
    map[stripped] = {
      adapter:     adapter,
      id:          idVal,
      keyOriginal: keyRaw,
      keyLow:      keyLow,
      stripped:    stripped
    };

    start = comma + 1;
  }
  return map;
}

// ============================================================================
// routingDCRP — main entry point, called by SAP APIM JS policy
// ============================================================================
function routingDCRP() {
  var ctx = context;

  // ── Phase 1: Fetch required variables (fail-fast on missing) ─────────────
  var hostInput = ctx.getVariable('target.cpi.host');
  var kvmInput  = ctx.getVariable('kvm.idinterface');
  var pathInput = ctx.getVariable('proxy.pathsuffix');

  if (!hostInput || !kvmInput || !pathInput) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error',
      'Missing:' +
      (hostInput ? '' : ' [target.cpi.host]') +
      (kvmInput  ? '' : ' [kvm.idinterface]') +
